questions.question: The principle of proportionality in EU law requires that:

options.A: EU institutions must act proportionately only in relation to member states, not private parties
options.B: Measures must not exceed what is appropriate and necessary to achieve the legitimate objective pursued
options.C: All EU legislation must be approved by a proportional share of member state populations
options.D: The Commission must apply measures in proportion to the size of member states' economies

questions.explanation: Proportionality (Article 5(4) TEU and as a general principle of EU law) requires that EU measures must be suitable to attain the objective, must be necessary (no less restrictive alternative is available), and must not impose burdens that are excessive in relation to the aims pursued. It applies to both EU institutions and member states when derogating from EU freedoms.

questions.question: The proportionality principle applies to review of EU institutions' acts. The Court of Justice in such cases applies:

options.A: A strict review, second-guessing all discretionary policy choices of the institutions
options.B: A relatively deferential review where complex economic and technical assessments are involved, checking only for manifest error, misuse of powers, or manifestly disproportionate measures
options.C: No review — institutional acts in the economic sphere are non-justiciable
options.D: A stricter standard when fundamental rights are at stake, requiring the most least-restrictive measure in all circumstances

questions.explanation: The Court applies varying intensity of proportionality review. For complex economic, social, or technical assessments, the Court is deferential — institutions have a broad margin of discretion and the Court reviews only for manifest error. Where fundamental rights are at stake, the Court applies a stricter, more intrusive proportionality analysis (as confirmed in cases like Huber v Germany C-524/06).

questions.question: In Fedesa [1990] (Case C-331/88), the Court of Justice applied proportionality to an EU Council Directive on hormones and held:

options.A: The Directive was disproportionate because it created an unnecessary regulatory burden on farmers
options.B: That where a complex economic assessment is involved, the legislature may not be found to have infringed proportionality unless the measure is manifestly inappropriate to achieve the objective
options.C: That proportionality requires automatic annulment of measures imposing financial burdens on producers
options.D: That the Directive had to be struck down because alternatives existed that would achieve the same aim

questions.explanation: In Fedesa, the Court confirmed that in areas involving complex economic and social policy assessments (here, agricultural and veterinary policy), the legislature has broad discretion and proportionality review is limited: the measure must only be manifestly inappropriate having regard to the objective. This is a deferential standard of review.

questions.question: The principle of legal certainty in EU law encompasses which of the following sub-principles?

options.A: The right of individuals to know the law and the prohibition on retrospective legislative measures that adversely affect individual legal positions
options.B: The requirement that all EU legislation be published in all official EU languages simultaneously
options.C: The right of member states to legislate on any matter not expressly addressed by EU law
options.D: The principle that the Court of Justice may not overrule its own previous judgments

questions.explanation: Legal certainty requires that the law be clear, precise, and predictable so that individuals can foresee the consequences of their actions. It encompasses: accessibility (law must be knowable); non-retroactivity (legislation should not adversely affect established legal positions); legitimate expectations; and the principle that laws must be applied consistently. These have all been recognised as aspects of the general principle by the Court of Justice.

questions.question: The principle of legitimate expectations protects:

options.A: Member states from changes in EU legislative policy that affect national revenue
options.B: Individuals who have relied on precise assurances given to them by authorised EU authorities that a particular course of action would be taken or lawful
options.C: EU institutions from being bound by previous judicial decisions in subsequent cases
options.D: National courts from having to apply EU law that conflicts with previously established national law

questions.explanation: Legitimate expectations protect those who have reasonably relied on a precise, unconditional representation or course of conduct by a competent EU authority. If the authority then acts contrary to that representation, those relying on it may have a remedy. The expectation must be legitimate — prudent traders are expected to know that rules may change, and cannot rely on perpetuation of every advantageous condition.

questions.question: Non-retroactivity as a sub-principle of legal certainty means:

options.A: EU law can never have any retroactive effect whatsoever
options.B: As a rule, EU measures may not apply to situations established before their entry into force, though genuine retroactivity may be permitted where the purpose requires it and legitimate expectations are respected
options.C: The Court of Justice may not give retroactive effect to its preliminary rulings
options.D: Member states may not implement EU Directives retroactively to the date of the Directive

questions.explanation: The Court of Justice recognises a general presumption against retroactivity (particularly for measures imposing burdens). However, the Court permits 'authentic retroactivity' in exceptional circumstances — where required by the objective and where legitimate expectations are adequately protected. Temporal limitation of preliminary rulings (limiting effects to future situations) also engages this principle.

questions.question: The three-stage proportionality test, as applied to member state measures restricting EU freedoms, requires:

options.B: Suitability (appropriate to achieve the aim), necessity (no less restrictive measure achieves the same aim), and proportionality stricto sensu (burdens not disproportionate to aims)
options.C: Legality, appropriateness, and consistency with general EU policy objectives
options.D: Non-discrimination, justification, and notification to the Commission

questions.explanation: The proportionality test as applied to member state restrictions on EU freedoms involves: (1) suitability/appropriateness — is the measure capable of achieving the aim?; (2) necessity — is there a less restrictive measure that achieves the same aim?; (3) proportionality stricto sensu — are the burdens imposed reasonable in relation to the objectives? Not all courts consistently apply all three stages, but the test is central to EU free movement law.

questions.question: The principle of equal treatment as a general principle of EU law requires:

options.A: All member states must have identical national laws
options.B: Comparable situations must not be treated differently, and different situations must not be treated in the same way, unless objectively justified
options.C: All EU citizens must receive identical benefits from member state social security systems
options.D: EU institutions must treat all legislative proposals from member states with equal priority

questions.explanation: The general principle of equal treatment (recognised by the Court as a fundamental principle of EU law) requires that comparable situations be treated alike and different situations differently, unless objective justification exists. It is distinct from the specific non-discrimination provisions in the Treaties and Charter but complements them.

questions.question: Legal certainty requires that EU penalties must be:

options.A: Applied only by member state courts, not EU institutions
options.B: Predictable, clearly defined in law before the prohibited act, and not retrospectively imposed — nullum crimen, nulla poena sine lege
options.C: Proportionate only to the economic harm caused by the infringement
options.D: Uniform across all member states to avoid distortions of competition

questions.explanation: The principle of legality in the criminal/penalty sphere (nulla poena sine lege — no punishment without law) is a fundamental principle of EU law, recognised explicitly in Article 49 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights. Penalties must be defined in sufficiently clear law before the conduct, and may not be applied retroactively to past conduct.

questions.question: In the context of EU competition law, the Commission's broad discretion in calculating fines is constrained by:

options.A: A mandatory ceiling of 10% of turnover under Regulation 1/2003, proportionality principles, and the requirement to state reasons
options.B: The Council's approval of each individual fine before it can be imposed
options.C: A requirement that all fines be of the same amount for the same category of infringement
options.D: The Court of Justice's binding precedent from previous competition penalty cases

questions.explanation: The Commission's discretion in setting fines for competition law infringements (under Regulation 1/2003) is legally constrained: there is a mandatory 10% of global turnover ceiling; fines must be proportionate to the gravity and duration of the infringement; and the Commission must provide adequate reasons for the level set. The Court reviews for proportionality and can reduce (but not increase) fines.
